speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --give away their pets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And of course, there was great hardship, you know. Children had to give their dogs, and people who lived with certain animals for many, many years. So-- but we, of course, were suspicious. And we very soon realized that it's the beginning of some deportations. First, we heard that they happened somewhere else. And the part of conquered Poland-- we heard that not only people were-- mass executions were taking place. Of course, we never knew what is true, what is exaggerated. We always hoped it is was exaggerated. It-- it was just living these rumors, which were terrible. And then came to the first news that people were deported eastward into the Ukraine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lodz was a new ghetto. And into this ghetto, they'll send transports of Jews from other countries. After the conquest of France-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was '43, no, '42, I think, '42-- first, all the foreign Jews were deported from France. And we-- we, of course-- you know, there was always an underground connection. So we heard it, but we heard it as rumors. There were Polish citizens-- students, business people-- who were settled in France, but had not the French citizenship. They were the first that were sent eastward. Where and what, we didn't know. Similar things came, then, from Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Foreigners from Germany were deported. But they made a point-- that it is not the people who lived there and belonged to the country, but foreigners.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: By rumors. But after that, of course, we could fill in and get the news. But-- now, my sister was in Holland and had to be very careful what she wrote in letters. And she started to talk about it. There was a camp built close to Amsterdam. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Forgot. And she heard about that and very soon was sent there-- Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Westerbork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Westerbork. And then, of course, I found out it was just a transition of stations. They actually would collect the Jews until they had enough for deportation to the east. We never knew anything else but to the east at the time. Also, that one letter that had come from Poland, from Auschwitz, was already a hint that something was going on. They would not make Jewish new settlements there. But it was a means to get rid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Oswiecim is the Polish name for Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: For Auschwitz, yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '42, a regular transportation from Jews to the east started. There was an enormous panic. All everybody could take was, I don't know, 25 kilos, or 50 pounds of the most important, which afterwards got lost anyway. And we were supposed to go east, where they would find a camp where they can survive the war, but nobody believed it anymore. But nobody clearly knew what happens. In the middle of-- I'll take a guess, now. I have the dates in my book somewhere-- of '43, middle-- beginning '43. They created a what they called ghetto in Theresien-- Theresienstadt, which was a small town south of Prague created by Maria Theresa for military. It was a fortification.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maria Theresa was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Kaiser in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: The wife of the Kaiser.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was-- Maria Theresa, she was the Emperor.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the Empress.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Empress, Maria Theresa, yeah. This is a-- and, of course, there were Czech soldiers, during the Czechoslovakia, in the camp. And then, it was taken over by the Germans-- particularly by the Gestapo, who had [INAUDIBLE] terrible-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't find the word-- [GERMAN], torture chambers there for-- for politcal-- politically suspected people. Now, they threw all the Czechs out, sent them into other areas to settle down. I cannot tell you. Maybe 10,000, 20,000 people lived there before. And-- and it made a place which they called a ghetto-- which was a concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People lived in one little home-- 20 people. Food was still available, but very rationed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you buy your food, or were you given your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were given your food twice a day. You got some piece of bread with coffee in the morning, and then, there was a meal in the later day. But there was still some illegal connection with the outside, and there was a black market where you could get a little more, or some people had even better food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your 50 pounds or 100 pounds--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --50 Pounds of your own stuff? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: --of our own stuff. We lived in this military camp on three levels. And, of course, there were more and more illnesses and weaknesses and people died. Old people couldn't survive there at all. They had--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got there by yourself, or with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get there? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were transported.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were transported, yeah. One day--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were put on a--

speaker: interviewee
text: One day at night, you got your order. You will be in the next transport, which is Monday at 8 o'clock in the morning. There was a place where you had to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] be early in the morning. You had to sign that you voluntarily leave your home, that you give everything to-- whatever they were called-- the German institution. They would take care of your possessions. You left your keys there. And then after a couple of days, when the number [INAUDIBLE] 1,000 was collected-- a few died, so they had to put someone else in-- got into the train, and we are transported, but not very far-- a few hours [? about. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: The train itself was a passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. You got into a cattle car. But there was still 50 people per wagon. It was-- it was impossible to survive. It was hot. It was stinky. It was unpleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say, because these were, of course, not scheduled trains. So they would push them a little bit and there, wait, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't days.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in, I would say in six, seven hours or so. We came through Theresienstadt-- [INAUDIBLE]-- where we first of all put our things were examined, whether we have anything that we shouldn't bring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like cigarettes. Cigarettes were forbidden, which meant that there was an enormous black market of cigarettes, because people just couldn't live without. But they died, because they had to give their bread for cigarettes that didn't have-- that many of them thought necessary. I was smoking until I entered the place in the park where we were collected. I threw out my last cigarette, and I remember it like today. It wasn't the last in my life, but the last before the war was over, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and-- because I had heard about what people suffered who cannot make it without cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made a conscious decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wouldn't give-- yeah, I wouldn't give my food for it, and nothing else I had. Of course, some people had some money smuggled in. That was kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any money that you smuggled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, we had some put into our clothes, yeah. Not much, but we didn't want to use it, because we didn't know what else will come and lost it when they took our-- our last possessions away later, and away to Auschwitz. So that was Theresien-- Theresienstadt. I survived very easily because I was a nearly finished doctor. I didn't have a license to practice, but I had finished my study and-- and wanted immediately to go in the hospital to work. And they said, we have too many doctors. If you want to work as a nurse, you are very welcome. As a matter of fact, I found a very nice professor who knew my name. I knew, at least, his name. He said, you know, I make a deal with it. If you work during the day as a nurse, you can work at night as a doctor. That was very important for my moral well-being. I got even a little bit more to eat, because we were exposed to infection, so they gave us an extra piece of bread and margarine. But that was not my only motivation. I worked very hard there. In daytime I was cleaning patients, putting bedpans around. And at night, I made service.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was wrong with most of the patients in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends. The younger people somehow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] survived. Most of them survived, unless they were sick and could not take care of their sickness. Diabetics had difficulties. Older people were hard to save. We had medication, but limited. We are just on the edge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Diabetic medicine? You had insulin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Insulin we had, but I don't know. Maybe we still had-- and then, some people had brought it with them, so it was not allowed. It could survive for some time. Later on, we didn't have it, and these people died. Old people got, usually, diarrhea from bad food, and the infection was just in the air. They had no way to go to a restroom. They had not the strength to walk out. Both grandmothers after a couple of weeks died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents fare?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My mother-- my mother worked in the potato cellar, cleaning potatoes. And could always take a couple of potatoes, or they give her a couple of potatoes, when she went out. So we made a little fire in the-- outside the barracks and cooked potatoes. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't everyone very jealous to see you cooking potatoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course they were jealous, but then they had theirs. You know, everybody-- there was not a great social feeling. You-- yes, with your family, of course, you divided what you had, as little as it may have been. But you knew that the person next has not.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that somebody next to you does not have anything, and you couldn't help it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, one potato for three people is not so much to eat that you can be generous. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was one difference. When you did hard work, and sometimes they sent out corps, even out of the camp to do hard work for the military, or-- or some German organization, you got somewhat more to eat. Not enough, but you got enough bread and margarine. It was usually--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had taken advantage of the courses that [INAUDIBLE] and I, and other people had organized. And he-- he always liked to learn. So he learned to-- optical work, and got very busy with this. You know, people lost their glasses. Now others were found on the street, trying to find glasses for people, who otherwise were practically blind. And he didn't get anything for it, but he was occupied. That was extremely important. Not just-- the other people just sit and wait for the piece of bread and soup in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: From March to September-- six months. And then, there came a rumor that now the transports from here east start. Of course, everybody tried not to get into it, which-- I had a friend who wasn't in the higher controls system-- Jewish-- leaders of the ghetto. I tried to find out, am I in a transport? Is-- are my parents in the transport? No, the next transport will be only younger people. Your parents will not be in, but you will be in. I had a secretary who worked up there and who was willing to do everything for me, had worked with me in Prague. And she tried to intervene for me. And she went up, says anytime, you bring me somebody who will go in his place, he can stay. Well, of course, that was like saying, sorry. I would not mention any names. They put him in instead. I was still a decent person at that time. Anyway, I was in the transport. The rumor was that you will be happy to get out of Theresienstadt, that it's terrible. There is hunger, the infections--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you finish with Theresienstadt, do you remember anything about the Red Cross coming in and making an inspection, and do you remember anything about Germans trying to make a better picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there anymore. I was gone already.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, I had heard about it, and I know enough. It was a big play act thing. It was something that in history has once happened in Spain and became a famous saying. It's a Spanish town where the King from-- God knows-- a friend of Spain came to visit. It was a terrible, poor, neglected area in Spain. And they built these fronts where they would bring the visitors. And they did it exactly in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Red Cross was not sophisticated enough really to-- of course, they didn't want to. They didn't want to find out. They wouldn't know-- I mean, against Hitler? Red Cross wanted to, at least, survive as an organization, as a military organization. They went there. They saw it. So, they went home again. But it was, really, a play, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a total play. People got clothes. They had to give it back afterwards. People got a good meal. Streets were cleaned. It was all worked out to the little detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was after you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: My leaving was very dramatic. It was on September 6, '43. They said, only younger people-- which then, let's see, that wasn't true. Whole families with children were in it too-- no old people. 5,000 will be sent to a new-- excuse me-- to a new place in the east, and you will create a new Theresienstadt [DRINKS] kind of ghetto which will be much richer, much better, in the countryside and will be the future of a way to survive. We didn't believe a word, but we liked at least to hope-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 5,000 people. That was a bad transport, because it was days and nights, and if you ask me how many, I don't know, but probably two nights, at least, and the days-- three days, four, whatever amount. With one bucket of water, and a little piece of bread that everybody had become-- would have not been able to eat the next day anyway, because we were so thirsty that there was no way to swallow it by. People were laying one over the other. There was buckets. Can't sit on the toilet, but, first of all, you couldn't reach it when you were not next to it. But then, it was full, so people just let go. It was just very bad. But I was young, and I survived it. Not everybody survived. Not too many people died, but it was torturous. And the most torturous thing was the thirst. You know, we were all dried out. You could not swallow your own saliva because you didn't have any more. It was really the worst transportation that I ever had to experience. And finally, we come to a place where the train stopped-- a small train station. And I look out the window. There was this one little window like in cattle cars, where you had to step on somebody else, or somebody had to help you up to look out, and I see the word Osviecim-- Auschwitz. Now I remember the letter. And I know I had only one thought and said it to my former secretary-- the woman that I had mentioned, who also was in the transport-- if they only would give us something to drink before they kill us. I mean, that was my last wish. I said it, of course, in a kind of half jokingly-- seriously joking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you romantically involved with this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not. But she obviously loved me, because she had volunteered to go in the transport when she couldn't take me out. No, I was not. I was sorry that I was not, because I know that she was devoted as a person only can be. She wrote a whole book for me that I had read during this time, and all kinds of things. Was willing to-- I mean, she would have sooner or later gone there anyway, but wanted to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She wanted to please you, and be with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think so. Maybe to please herself to be with me, help me, if it's possible. Was a very-- this woman was married to a Czech pilot, who was in the American-- no, in the Czech air force, prior to the invasion of the Germans, and flew with his airplane to England and became a part of the Czech squadron of [INAUDIBLE] pilots.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you were, and you saw the name Oswiecim, which meant Auschwitz, and then what happened in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened the-- the cars, and the famous screaming starts, out, out, out! Fast! Run! Leave everything there. You get it later. Of course, we never saw any of our luggage or anything. And-- and what usually then happens, didn't happen. Of course, we didn't know what usually happened. What usually happens is the selection, where they select people who are fit for work from the rest. The whole 5,000 people, as we were, were taken into one sector of Auschwitz. And there were the old-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] usually a few from-- people who were already there for two or three years, working, building these camps and say, we haven't experienced this-- this before. You are some special people that they don't select the good ones from the bad ones right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't know what they were talking about. So we asked, were there transports before? He said, of course. Where are they? And they usually show up to the sky, and say, up there. Later, they showed the smoke in the background, and they would say, you see the smoke? This is the way you go out of this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't believe it. We couldn't understand it. We thought they want to frighten us so that we would be willing to do anything they are saying. Even though they were-- we didn't know yet what they were, but they were people like us. Only they were already the old guard who had certain positions and survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were pushed all in in one sector. It was B-2-B. One side was A, and the other side was C, the camp full of Gypsies. And there was D, a men's camp, and F, a hospital camp. So, that meant A, B, C, D, E, F. That whole area was called Birkenau-- it was part of Auschwitz-- because there were a few birches there on a little hill. There were birches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birches, yeah. And Birkenau means birch-- little forest. Forest is already too much for it-- four or five birches standing on a hill. So they called this area Birkenau, which was the closest to the gas chambers and the closest to the crematorium. And there we were, 5,000 people-- men, women. So the men went to one side. The women went to barracks on the other side. And in the middle was mud, because it was already September-- raining time, and so-- which was called the camp road-- the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So barracks on one side. Barracks on the other side. And a road in the middle. Women with children had a special-- children could stay with-- with their mothers in a special barrack. I don't know how many it was. This was September 6, '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The next-- there are many little things I could tell you, but we don't have nearly the time for. What was most important is that after a while, a man came-- a sergeant, a German sergeant came and said, all doctors, step out. There were quite a number of doctors there. They stepped out. I stepped out carefully. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said, you stand there and don't you move. The chief, German chief, doctor of this camp will come and talk to you. And I was standing a little bit separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had a terrible decision to make. Sure, I was finished with my medical studies and had worked as a doctor, but I didn't have my diploma, because that was when the Germans came in and cut me off. So, am I a doctor, or am I not a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there came on a motorcycle a high SS man running-- I mean riding in so the dust-- looked all at us. We looked terrible after the transport. We looked really like some beggars afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saw me standing a little bit up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] away from the rest. What's the matter with you? And I said, I-- I am a doctor, but I have not gotten my diploma. Everybody laughed. And he said you go to the hospital and work there, so you won't forget your medicine.
